TextENGNew: Welcome.
Welcome to the world of Higurashi: When They Cry's final chapters — Matsuribayashi and Miotsukushi.
In this world, you will be able to reconstruct the story's world from Hanyuu's perspective.
Your ultimate goal is to bring about the ideal June of 1983 — the one you most wish to see.
To make that ideal a reality, you must gather many different Fragments.
However, Takano's ideal is equally unyielding.
changeReason: Old translates 理想 (ideal) as 'conviction'; new correctly renders it as 'ideal', changing the characterization of Takano's motivation.

TextENGNew: To overturn it, considerable effort and a great many Fragments will be required.
I warn you now — this task is an extraordinarily slow and painful one.
Hanyuu spent over a hundred years at this very task — and even she, in the end, faltered and surrendered herself to fate.
Overcoming Takano's ideal is no easy feat.
changeReason: Same correction as above: 理想 rendered as 'ideal' instead of 'conviction', altering what must be overcome.

TextENGNew: The trials of this world will subject you to suffering not unlike what Hanyuu herself endured.
Ground down by the endless repetition of worlds spun from countless Fragments, you may find your spirit breaking — and surrender, just as Hanyuu once did.
Should you wish to surrender —
you may end this game at any time.
In that case, the worlds surrounding you and your companions' fates will close with Onikakushi and end at Minagoroshi.
changeReason: New translation adds 'you and' before companions, matching the Japanese あなたとあなたの仲間たち (you and your companions), which the old translation omitted.

TextENGNew: By forgetting this world — by erasing all that you have witnessed from your memory — you will meet your death within it.
changeReason: Old says 'you will finally accept death'; new says 'you will meet your death within it'. The Japanese specifies この世界での死 (death in this world), a locational detail the old translation drops.

TextENGNew: ...Just as Rika eventually did.
Your ideal June of 1983 will take shape by joining together ninety-nine Fragments scattered across this world.
To join the Fragments, you will need to find the ones that correspond to each other.
...Ninety-nine of them — a sheer volume that may well leave you dizzy.
But this world is not without mercy.
To ease your suffering, however slightly, a small measure of guidance will be offered.
When you select a Fragment that cannot yet be joined —
your one and only ally here will mark that Fragment to show it cannot yet be joined.
So long as that mark remains, the Fragment cannot be connected.
It would be best to go search for other Fragments in the meantime.
But as you join other Fragments together, you may find that a Fragment which could not be connected before has, without you realizing it, become possible to join.
When that happens, she will mark it to let you know.
You should try joining those Fragments right away.
...In doing so, yet another Fragment may become available to connect.
There are also Fragments among them that will offer you counsel.
They will make themselves known through the "words of Fragments" you have gathered from the worlds traversed until now.
...Or perhaps entirely different words.
...Yes — the words most deeply familiar to you from playing Higurashi: When They Cry itself may sometimes open the way.
Trying many different things is itself one avenue forward——.
And when all ninety-nine Fragments have at last been joined and you reach the Fragment of Matsuribayashi — or of Miotsukushi —@k ...your ideal June of 1983 will be born.
That is the moment when Matsuribayashi — or Miotsukushi — the chapter we have yearned for most — will finally begin.
Before examining each Fragment one by one, it would be wise to first survey all ninety-nine laid out before you.
Since most Fragments require other Fragments before they can be joined, the number you will be able to examine at first is only a handful.
Let us begin by searching for those.
...This will be an extraordinarily long and arduous trial.
I say it again: Hanyuu confronted this challenge for over a hundred years, and even she broke under it.
If you hold within you the same faith in miracles that your companions carry —
Even if you do not inhabit Hanyuu's world — if you are able to believe in them...
Now... the Fragment-Weaving begins — the gathering of fate's shattered pieces, scattered and adrift.
It is a vital and demanding journey — one that will put the full strength of your will to the test.
In this world, you will be tested: do you believe in the world that Rika and the others believed in?
A miracle comes only when everyone's strength is gathered together.
The June of 1983 that Takano has constructed is fearfully fortified.
changeReason: Old: 'nearly indomitable'; New: 'fearfully fortified'. Japanese 強固 means 'strongly built/fortified' rather than 'indomitable', shifting the characterization from unstoppability to structural strength.

TextENGNew: She has burrowed into the core of the nation's power, secured powerful backing, and accumulated forces far beyond what any ordinary person could command.
changeReason: Old implies Takano gathered powerful people to her side; new says she infiltrated existing power structures. Japanese 中枢に入り込んで (burrowed into the core) supports the new reading as active infiltration.

TextENGNew: The odds of overcoming her are lower than rolling ones on a hundred dice simultaneously.
In other words, there is no path to victory that does not pass through a miracle.
And a miracle comes only when everyone's strength is joined as one.
And that everyone — includes you.
Furthermore, for those who have finished reading these stories, yet more worlds await.
...One is Saikoroshi.
This is the world that Rika, above all others, wished never to see.
But to confirm her true self and the growth she has achieved, this world must not be avoided.
...And the other is Kotohogushi.
This is a world that Hanyuu herself sealed away — from the past, and from her own memory.
The age when Hinamizawa was still known as Onigafuchi.
There you will be able to glimpse one chapter in how she came to be called Oyashiro-sama...
Though her own memories have been considerably distorted after so many years of wandering — so not everything there can be taken as wholly accurate.
With that in mind, I hope you will enjoy these as worlds that "probably" once were.
changeReason: Old says 'enjoy this world as it is' omitting the key epistemic qualifier のであろう (probably were) and using singular 'world' vs plural 'worlds' — changes the meta-narrative framing.

TextENGNew: ...Are you ready?
Of course I am.
The secret to a long journey is knowing not to push yourself too hard.
Let's take it easy.
I'll be watching from my usual spot.
Shall we begin?
We have all the time in this world.
Let us meet again when you have safely seen every last world through to its end.
...Hee hee.
